title: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 09 to 26 by Livy
paragraphs: Papirius Cursor soon after eradicated this shame by defeating the Samnites, forcing them into submission, and retrieving the hostages. When ambassadors who had been sent to make restitution returned without concluding a peace, he said: "Whatever degree of anger the gods of heaven had conceived against us because of the infraction of the treaty, has now been expiated." He led out the troops after making those predictions, and set up camp near Caudium as far from view as possible.

The lieutenants-general and tribunes, summoned without consultation, gathered around the dejected consul. There was no room for either consultation or remedy. The soldiers, crowding to the general's quarters, demanded succour from their leaders, which it was hardly possible for even the gods themselves to provide. 3 By what road will you reach the enemy? The Samnites, despite their joyful circumstances, were unable to make a decision immediately. It was therefore universally agreed that Herennius Pontius, the general's father, should be consulted by letter.

When these answers, so opposite to each other, like those of an ambiguous oracle, were given, it was decided that despite his son's belief that his father's mind and body had been impaired by age, he should still be consulted due to the general desire of all. He intended to achieve two objectives through his plans. Firstly, he aimed to establish perpetual peace and friendship with a most powerful nation through an extraordinary act of kindness. Secondly, he sought to delay the return of war for many ages, thereby allowing the Roman state sufficient time to recover from the loss of its two armies. "There was a third plan." When his son and the other chiefs went to ask him if "a plan of a middle kind might not be adopted; that they both should be dismissed unhurt, and at the same time, by the right of war, terms imposed upon them as vanquished." "That's indeed a plan of such a nature that neither procures friends nor removes enemies."

Let us therefore endure whatever indignity may come, no matter how great it is, and let us submit to the necessity that even the gods cannot overcome. For what occasion would these be either for sureties or hostages? In the former case, where ratification is performed by the imprecation, "that whichever nation shall give occasion to the said terms being violated, may Jupiter strike that nation in like manner as the swine is struck by the heralds." The consuls, lieutenants-general, quaestors, and military tribunes became sureties. The names of all those who became sureties are recorded. If the business had been conducted through a treaty, only the names of the two heralds would have appeared. The return of the consuls renewed the general grief in the camp. The men hardly refrained from offering violence to them. They said that their rashness had brought them to this point, and that it was only through cowardice that they would likely depart with greater shame than they had arrived.

They had been sent out without a guide or scouts, but were forced to proceed blindly, like animals into a trap. They cast nervous glances at each other, studying their arms that would soon be surrendered; their bodies would be at the mercy of the enemy. They imagined the hostile yoke, the mocking jeers of the conquerors, and the haughty looks of their captors. Finally, disarmed and vulnerable, they marched through the midst of an armed foe. They were given arms, they were given strength, they were given courage. While they were indulging in such profound reflections, the fatal hour of their downfall arrived, rendering every circumstance even more shocking in reality than they had anticipated. Next day, when some young nobles who had been sent to escort them on their road to the frontiers of Campania returned, they were summoned into the senate-house. In response to the inquiries of the elder members, they reported that "to them it seemed that the young nobles appeared deeply sunk in melancholy and dejection; the entire body moved forward in silence, almost as if they were mute. The former Roman spirit had been lost, and their will to fight had been taken from them, along with their arms."

They initially learned that the troops were confined. Later, the news of the shameful peace caused even more suffering than they had experienced during their peril. When the senate passed its customary decrees, they proposed considering the Caudine peace. Publilius, who held the fasces, said, "Spurius Postumius, speak." He rose with the same countenance he had worn when passing under the yoke and declared: "Consuls, I am aware that I was summoned first in ignominy, not honor, and that I am ordered to speak as a senator's equal, accountable for both an unsuccessful war and a dishonorable peace." I pray and beseech that, although it has not been your will that Spurius Postumius and Titus Veturius, as consuls, should wage war with success against the Samnites, you may yet deem it sufficient to have seen us sent under the yoke; to have seen us bound by an infamous convention; to have seen us delivered into the hands of our foes naked and shackled, taking on ourselves the whole weight of their resentment.

To Postumius replied, "In the meantime, surrender us as unsanctified persons, which you can do without offending religion. Those sacred and inviolable personages, the tribunes, will be delivered up to you afterwards, once they leave office. But if you listen to me, they will first be scourged with rods here in the Comitium, as a penalty for their delayed punishment." For, as to their denying that the people are acquitted of the religious obligation by our being given up, who is there so ignorant of the laws of heraldry, as not to know that those men speak in that manner, that they themselves may not be surrendered, rather than because the case is really so. We should not discuss the surrender further, as becoming sureties was the key point emphasized.

We acted with insufficient caution during the war. On the other hand, they threw away a victory that we had obtained through our own folly. They hardly trusted the places from which they had conquered, but instead rushed to take arms from the hands of men who were born for battle. Would it have been difficult, had their reason been sound, to send ambassadors to Rome and negotiate a peace and treaty with the senate and the people during the time they spent sending for old men from home to give them advice. You are therefore in no way concerned either with us, to whom you gave no commission; nor with the Samnites, with whom you had no business.

Let us, Titus Veturius, and those concerned, offer ourselves as atonement for breaking our engagements. By our suffering, we aim to liberate the Roman armies. " 10 As soon as the decree of the senate was passed, it seemed as if a new light had shone upon the state. Postumius was everyone's talk: they extolled him to heaven; and declared his conduct equal to that of the consul Publius Decius' devoting act, and other illustrious acts. "The state had risen to prominence through his counsel and efforts." He now offered himself to the enemy's wrath, and to their torments; and was suffering, as atonement for the Roman people. "When will we be allowed to meet the Samnites with arms in our hands?" While the state was consumed by resentment and anger, it was largely governed by volunteers.

Pontius then said, "Neither will I accept such a surrender, nor will the Samnites deem it valid. You ransomed your state from the Gauls for gold, but while they were receiving that gold, they were put to the sword. It is only fair that you should obtain the safety of so many of your countrymen as a result of this convention, whereas I am not granted the peace I stipulated for when sending back those men."

The gods are believed to have decreed that Postumius is a citizen of Samnium, not Rome. A Roman ambassador was attacked by a Samnite, which led to a justified war being waged against us by you. The Romans viewed their ability to wage war as a certain victory, whereas the Samnites believed that if the Romans resumed their arms, they would be victorious. The Satricans rebelled against the Samnites, who then attacked the colony of Fregellae with a surprise assault at night.

From that time until day, their mutual fears kept both parties quiet. The daylight was the signal for battle, which the Fregellans contrived to maintain for a considerable time without loss of ground. They fought for their religion and liberty, and the multitude, who were unfit to bear arms, assisted them from the tops of the houses. A part, more determined, however, retained their arms and rushed out by the opposite gate. Their boldness brought greater safety to them than their fear, which inclined them to credulity, did to the others. For the Samnites, having surrounded the latter with fires, burned them all to death while they made vain appeals to the faith of gods and men. They decided it was best to rely on fate's decision and engage in battle with Publilius; thus, they deployed their forces into the field. 13

They advanced therefore to battle, urging the standard-bearers to hasten. In beginning the conflict, there should be no delay. As they wielded their javelins, they drew their swords. Throwing away the former as if a signal had been given, they rushed in full speed upon the foe. The enemy, therefore, were not only completely routed. They did not even dare to retreat to their camp, but instead dispersed and made their way towards Apulia in scattered parties. Afterward, they collected their forces into one body and arrived at Lucera. The same exasperation that had driven the Romans through the midst of the enemy's line now propelled them forward into their camp, where they inflicted greater carnage and spilled more blood than ever before, despite the fact that much of the spoils were lost to their fury.

The Samnites, encamped at Luceria, had little hope of survival. With no prospect of relief, they were forced to gather what remaining strength they could from all directions and prepare for a final confrontation with Papirius. 14 On the other side, the Samnites, who had neglected every preparation for fighting, either because they were genuinely desirous of peace or it seemed to them that pretending to be so would conciliate the favour of the Tarentines. When they saw the Romans drawn up for battle on a sudden, they cried out, "We will continue to be directed by the Tarentines and will neither march out nor carry our arms beyond the rampart." I think it is more strange that there should exist any doubt whether Lucius Cornelius was the dictator who performed these achievements at Caudium, and afterwards at Luceria, as the single avenger of the Romans' disgrace, enjoying the best-deserved triumph, perhaps next to that of Furius Camillus.

The city, to which their army had retreated after its defeat, surrendered on terms, with hostages being demanded. The other consul conducted his operations against the Satricans, who, despite being Roman citizens, rebelled against the Samnites following their defeat at Caudium and received a garrison in their city. The Satricans, however, when the Roman army approached their walls, sent deputies to sue for peace, with humble entreaties. The consul responded harshly, saying they must not come again unless they either put to death or deliver up the Samnite garrison. This response struck greater terror into the colonists than the threat of arms itself.

Two factions divided the senate: one led by those who had authored the defection from the Roman people, while the other comprised citizens loyal to Rome. Both groups shared a sincere intention to employ all necessary means to achieve an accommodation with the consul in order to restore peace. He possessed extraordinary swiftness of foot, surpassing everyone of his age in running. This remarkable ability led to the origin of his surname within his family. Additionally, it is said that either due to his robust frame or extensive practice, he was able to consume a substantial amount of food and wine. Neither the foot soldier nor the horseman felt military service more laborious than anyone else under a general, for he was of a constitution that could not be overcome by fatigue.

The praetor of Praeneste, fearing for his men's safety, had been slow to bring them forward from the reserve to the front. As he walked before his tent, he ordered his men to be called and instructed the lictor to prepare his axe. The Praenestine, standing frozen with fear, was told to cut away a troublesome stump that obstructed people's path as they walked. With this, the praetor heightened the man's anxiety about facing severe punishment. He then imposed a fine and dismissed him. It is beyond doubt that during that age, than which none was ever more productive of virtuous characters, there was no man whose Roman affairs found a more effectual support. People even marked him out as a match for Alexander the Great, in case he had completed the conquest of Asia and turned his arms on Europe. 17 The same fate may have befallen the Decii, who, having devoted themselves to the gods, charged into battle with reckless abandon; or Papirius Cursor, despite possessing extraordinary abilities in both body and mind.

He would have owned it if he were dealing with Darius, who drew after him a train of women and eunuchs. All he saw was gold and purple; he was encumbered by the trappings of his state, making him nothing more than prey rather than an opponent. He vanquished him without loss of blood, and his only merit on that occasion was showing proper spirit in despising empty show. If (Note: The original text appears to be a passage from "The History of the Peloponnesian War" by Thucydides. It describes how a person's behavior after success might differ from their previous actions.) It was common for several armies to be deployed simultaneously. In Etruria and Umbria, multiple forces clashed with the Gauls, who were also engaged in conflict in Samnium and Lucania.

It would have been a burden for him to bring Persians and Indians, along with the rest of the nations. The Romans, being at home, would have had recruits readily available. In contrast, Alexander, who waged war in a foreign land, would have found his army exhausted from prolonged service, an experience that later befell Hannibal. In truth, even if events had been favourable to him at first, he would have often wished for the Persians, Indians, and effeminate tribes of Asia as opponents. He would acknowledge that his wars were fought against women, as Alexander, king of Epirus, said after receiving his mortal wound, when comparing the wars waged in Asia by this youth with those he himself had been engaged in.

Ambassadors came from most of the states of the Samnites to renew the treaty. After moving the compassion of the senate by prostrating themselves before it, they were referred to the people and found their prayers ineffective. The treaty, therefore, having been refused, was subsequently obtained after they had individually petitioned them for several days. They pledged themselves boldly and received a grant of alliance, not on equal terms, but by submitting to the authority of the Roman people.

They formed a regular attack, relying on mutual support rather than their individual strength, and pressed on against the Romans. He directed his main efforts against those who had left the town, and with little resistance, drove them back inside the walls. As he rode easily among the horsemen, urging them to fight, a Roman master of the horses charged towards him, spear in hand, and struck with such force that he fell from his horse, dead.

They, however, were not, as is generally the case, dismayed by the fall of their leader, but rather rose to fury. I will not, therefore, lead you astray by abandoning this camp, from which you can retreat, as we did on a previous day, without securing the victory. While the city was in a dire situation, no certain mode of attack could be devised, and success must either be distant in time or at great risk. A deserter from Sora emerged from the town under cover of night and requested to be escorted immediately to the consuls. His request was granted, and he offered to deliver the city into their hands.

After posting the Romans here, he showed them a steep and narrow path leading up from the town to the citadel. "From this ascent," he said, "even three well-armed men would deter any large crowd." Sora was now taken. At dawn, the consuls arrived and accepted the surrender of those who remained after the flight and slaughter of the previous night. The Ausonian nation fell under Roman power, following the example of Sora, when their cities were betrayed. These included Ausona's Minturnae and Vescia.

The Romans had captured Luceria at the beginning of the year, and it soon fell into the hands of the Samnites. The colonists should be sent. Accordingly, 250 were transported there. The inquiry did not continue for long due to the illustrious names of the accused. This was a common phenomenon, as new inquiries often followed in their wake. However, as time passed, the investigation began to focus on individuals of lower social standing. Eventually, it was brought to an end by the very factions and cabals that had initially sparked its creation. 27

They therefore marched into the field, placing their cavalry on both wings and giving orders to pay closer attention to the camp behind than to the battle, as the line of infantry could protect its own safety. The arrival of the consul brought about a swift change in every aspect. Upon seeing their leader, the soldiers' spirits were revitalized, and the bravery of those who accompanied him made them an even more formidable force than their numbers; meanwhile, news of success on the other wing, which was initially heard but later confirmed, restored the fight. The consuls, after this important victory, led the legions to lay siege to Bovianum. There, they spent the winter quarters until Caius Poetelius was nominated dictator. With Marcus Foslius, master of the horse, he received command of the army from the new consuls, Lucius Papirius Cursor and Caius Junius Bubulcus.

Having surveyed the city's situation, he aimed to make the approach to the fortifications more open. To achieve this, he set fire to all the numerous buildings surrounding the walls. Shortly thereafter, Nola fell, either under the dictator Poetelius or the consul Caius Junius, as both accounts are reported. While one of the consuls prosecuted the remainder of the war in Samnium, Publius Decius, who was severely ill and stayed in Rome, was ordered by the Senate to appoint Caius Junius Bubulcus as dictator. The censorship of Appius Claudius and Caius Plautius this year was remarkable. However, it is Appius Claudius whose name has been passed down to posterity with greater fame, largely due to his construction of the road, which bore his name—the Appian Way—and for bringing water into the city.

A circumstance is recorded, wonderful to be told, and one which should make people scrupulous of disturbing established religious modes: for though there were twelve branches of the Potitian family at that time, all grown-up persons, yet they were cut off within a year; so that the name of the Potitii became extinct. 30 The flute players, taking offense because they had been prohibited by the last censors from holding their banquets in the temple of Jupiter, which had been customary since very early times, went off in a body to Tibur; so that there was not one left in the city to play at the sacrifices. They were thrown into wagons and taken to Rome. They knew nothing about it until the wagons were left in the forum, and they awoke, still severely ill from the previous night's debauchery. People gathered around them, and after they finally agreed to stay, permission was given for them to roam freely about the city dressed in full attire, accompanied by music.

These incidents took place during a time when public attention was deeply engaged with two of the most significant conflicts. 31 In Samnium, the Samnites blockaded and starved Cluvia, a Roman garrison, because they were unable to capture it by force. After subjecting the surrendering townspeople to cruel treatment, including flogging, they executed those who yielded. As an unexpected event unfolded, confusion arose while they loaded their arms and threw their baggage into the center. However, once each individual had freed himself from his burden and equipped himself with arms, they gathered around the standards from all sides. Given their extensive experience and knowledge of their respective ranks, the line formed spontaneously without any need for further direction.

While the situation in Samnium was such that all the states of Etruria, except the Arretians, had taken up arms and launched vigorous hostilities, they laid siege to Sutrium. The city, allied with Rome, acted as a buffer zone against Etruria. The Etruscans, due to their front line being unsupported by fresh reserves, fell all around and before the standards. In no battle would there have been seen less disposition to run or a greater effusion of human blood had not the night provided shelter for the resolute Etruscans, who were determined on death. It was thus the victors, rather than the vanquished, who first desisted from fighting. As dusk fell, the order to withdraw was issued, and both sides retreated into the darkness of night to their respective encampments.

While Appius urged such frivolous arguments as these, which carried no conviction whatever, another Roman said, "Behold, Romans, the son of Appius, who was appointed decemvir for one year. He then became a second decemvir by his own authority. During his third term, without being created by anyone or even himself, he held power over the fasces and government as a private individual. He continued in office until the government, which had been poorly acquired, poorly administered, and poorly retained, ultimately led to his downfall." They all knew it, certainly. As a result, they obeyed the Aemilian law instead of the old one, which had initially given rise to the creation of censors. This was because the Aemilian law was the last order in place, and when two laws are contradictory, the new one supersedes the old one. It bound all censors thereafter, for a century.

It is foolish to assume that someone who has performed crucial duties and stepped down as dictator within a 20-day period would be viewed as stupid. Similarly, it is illogical to believe that an individual who was irregularly appointed would resign from their position. With respect to what I have hitherto said, your pride and obstinacy compelled me to speak. Although six of the tribunes approved of their colleague's proceeding, three gave their support to Appius after he appealed to them. He then held the censorship alone, causing great discontent among all ranks of men. 35

While such was the state of affairs at Rome, the Etrurians had laid siege to Sutrium. The consul Fabius, marching along the foot of the mountains with a design to succour the allies and attempt the enemy's works if practicable, encountered their army prepared for battle. It is said that he had only one attendant, a slave who had been raised alongside him. The slave was therefore well-versed in the same language. The Romans ventured as far as the Camertian district of the Umbrians. There, they were introduced to the senate and treated with them in the name of the consul regarding an alliance and friendship. After being entertained with courteous hospitality, they were asked to inform the Romans that if they entered those countries, provisions would be ready for thirty days' worth of troops. The youth of the Camertian Umbrians were prepared in arms to obey their commands.

They came therefore to Sutrium, with an army as numerous as they had ever seen before. They did not venture to encamp on the outside of the wood, but instead marched down the plains to offer battle as soon as possible. He then addressed a few words to them; spoke highly of the wars of the Samnites and disparaged the Etrurians, saying they were not comparable as enemies or a numerically significant force among others. They awoke refreshed and immediately went to bed again.

Some were put to death before they could stir. Others lay half asleep in their beds. The greatest part ran in confusion to arms while others had little time to arm themselves. Those who followed no particular leader or orders were quickly routed by the Romans and pursued by their horses. During these transactions in Etruria, the other consul, Caius Marcius Rutilus, captured Allifae from the Samnites. Many of their forts and smaller towns were either destroyed by his forces or surrendered without resistance. Immediately on landing, the soldiers set out to ravage the country around Nuceria. They quickly laid waste to the parts nearest their position, from which they could have returned to the ships with safety. However, tempted by plunder, they advanced too far, provoking the enemy and drawing them against them. While traveling through the countryside, they encountered no resistance, although they may have been cut off at any moment. As they returned, in a carefree manner, the peasants intercepted them, not far from the shore, stripping them of their spoils and slaughtering a significant portion of them.

Although both parties suffered severely, it was the discredit that fell on the Romans. Several equestrians of high rank, including military tribunes and a lieutenant-general, had fallen in battle. What was most remarkable was that the consul himself was also wounded. Papirius appointed Caius Junius Bubulcus master of the horse. As he proceeded to an assembly of the Curiae to obtain an order regarding the command of the army, an unfortunate omen compelled him to adjourn it. The Curia that was to vote first happened to be the Faucian, a Curia marked by two significant disasters: the capture of the city and the Caudine peace. They stood drawn up for battle and under arms, waiting for nightfall. Neither side chose to engage.

They camped for a considerable time near each other, without taking action; neither confident in their own strength, nor disdaining the enemy. Meanwhile, matters continued to unfold in Etruria. A decisive battle was fought against the Umbrians, in which the enemy was defeated and suffered relatively few casualties, as they were unable to sustain the intensity of their initial attack. Equal danger and a glorious issue soon followed the war with the Samnites. They, in addition to their numerous preparations for battle, adorned their armor with new decorations.

The brightest ornament of a soldier was valour. All those trinkets would follow victory, and those rich enemies would be valuable prizes to the conquerors, regardless of their wealth. Cursor, having animated his men with these observations, led them into battle. The struggle between the two armies became desperate as soon as they engaged. The situation was equally dire for the dictator and the commander of the cavalry, whose wing would be the first to demonstrate victory. It was Junius who first took the left wing, forcing the enemy to yield. This comprised men adhering to the traditional Samnite custom, recognizable by their white garments and matching armor.

The customs of the Roman people were influenced by their festivals and public events. The aediles, who were responsible for maintaining the city's infrastructure and organizing public works, took it upon themselves to decorate the forum with elaborate displays during the grand processions that accompanied the great games. The consul Fabius fought with the remnants of the Etruscans at Perusia, where the city had also broken its truce, securing a decisive victory. The people of Alfaterna, after marching to Nuceria, rejected an offer of peace made by the latter, as they had not previously accepted it. The people were subsequently forced to surrender through the use of force.

Deputies were therefore despatched to the consul Fabius with directions that, if he had any respite from the war against the Samnites, he should with all haste lead his army into Umbria. However, one district, called Materina by themselves, prevailed on the rest not only to retain their arms but to come to an immediate engagement. They fell upon Fabius while he was fortifying his camp.

After his election to the consulship, a new war was decreed against the Sallentines by his colleague. He remained in Rome, intending to increase his influence through city politics, as the means of gaining honor in war had been entrusted to others. It had been invested before nightfall, and it was guarded until morning. They proposed to surrender on the next morning, despite the barely discernible light. It was agreed that those from Samnium would be allowed to leave with only a single garment each.

Calatia and Sora, along with the Roman garrisons stationed there, were captured. Extreme cruelty was inflicted on the captive soldiers. Publius Cornelius was subsequently sent to the area with an army. In the beginning, the consuls secured all passes between their camps in such a way that no messenger, regardless of expertise, could travel from one camp to the other. As a result, each consul spent several days in complete uncertainty about matters and was filled with anxiety over the other consul's state. The conduct of the Hernicians during the course of the war afterwards showed nothing suitable to the present alarm or to the ancient renown of that nation.

Without ever venturing any effort worth mentioning, three different camps were stripped within a few days. They stipulated for a truce of thirty days, during which they might send representatives to Rome, to the senate, on the terms of furnishing two months' pay and corn, along with a tunic to every soldier. In Samnium, the other consul, despite being stronger in numbers, was deeply troubled by his circumstances; the enemy had blocked all roads and occupied the passable defiles, cutting off supplies. The consul, although he regularly led his troops out to offer battle, could not persuade them to engage. After a slaughter of thirty thousand men, the consuls gave the signal for retreat and began collecting their forces into one body. As they did so, some new cohorts of the enemy, which had been levied as reinforcements, appeared on the horizon, prompting a renewed bout of bloodshed.

To three states of the Hernicians — the Alatrians, Verulans, and Ferentines — their own laws were restored because they preferred these to being made citizens of Rome. They were permitted to intermarry with each other, a privilege they alone of the Hernicians enjoyed for a long time after. Caius Junius Bubulcus, as censor, commissioned the construction of a temple dedicated to Health, a vow he had made during his consulship while fighting against the Samnites. The treaty with the Carthaginians was renewed for the third time in the same year, and generous gifts were presented to their ambassadors who attended this meeting. 44

By these means, the election of consuls was held, for it was their purpose that they had been created, since neither of the consuls could be absent from the armies. The consuls elected were Lucius Postumius and Titus Minucius; whom Piso places next after Quintus Fabius and Publius Decius, omitting the two years in which I have placed Claudius with Volumnius, and Cornelius with Marcius, as consuls. During this year, the Samnites made incursions into the district of Stellae in the Campanian territory.

Both consuls were sent into Samnium and proceeded to different regions: Postumius to Tifernum, Minucius to Bovianum. The next day, Bovianum was besieged and soon after captured. During the same year, Sora, Arpinum, and Censennia were recovered from the Samnites.

One proposition that stood out was that individuals should prioritize their own interests over the greater good. They discovered their city's design through scouting and subsequently attacked it, one after another. In a span of fifty days, they seized forty-one towns, most of which were destroyed and burned. The Aequan people were nearly exterminated as a result. I find in some ancient records that while attending the aediles, I witnessed him being elected aedile by the prerogative tribe. However, when it was discovered that he had acted as a notary, his name was not accepted. In response, he threw down his tablet and swore an oath to never pursue that line of work in the future.

To the great displeasure of the nobles, he performed the dedication of the temple of Concord in the area of Vulcan's temple. The chief pontiff, Cornelius Barbatus, was compelled by the united instances of the people to dictate to him the form of words. However, he affirmed that, consistently with the practice of antiquity, only a consul or commander-in-chief could dedicate a temple. The annual review of the knights was held on the Ides of July.

In a battle against the combined forces of the Etruscans, Umbrians, Samnites, and Gauls, Publius Decius, following in his father's footsteps, dedicates himself to the army. During the consulship of Lucius Genucius and Servius Cornelius, the state enjoyed almost uninterrupted peace from foreign wars. The Frusinonians were fined a third part of their lands because it was discovered that the Hernicians had been tampered with by them. The heads of this conspiracy, after being tried before the consuls in accordance with a senatorial decree, were punished by being beaten with rods and beheaded.

The Romans did not pass the entire year without war. A small expedition was sent to Umbria to gather intelligence. From there, it was learned that men on armed excursions had ventured into an adjacent country from a specific cave. They caused an alarm at Rome, which led to Caius Junius Bubulcus being nominated dictator to quell the insurrection. It was hardly believable that the Aequans, after being so weakened, would attempt to wage war on their own. A fleet of Greeks, led by Cleonymus, a Spartan, arrived on the coast of Italy and captured Thuriae, a city in the territory of the Sallentines.

He then sailed around the promontory of Brundusium, steering down the middle of the Adriatic Gulf. He did so because he feared the coasts of Italy, which lacked harbors, and the Illyrians, Liburnians, and Istrians on his right, who were known for piracy. When news of this reached Patavium, the Gauls' proximity kept the inhabitants in a state of constant readiness. The young men were divided into two groups: one led towards the area where marauders were said to be operating; the other took a different route, heading towards the ship station, fifteen miles from the town. Various causes of apprehension subsequently arose.

Marcus Valerius Maximus was nominated dictator due to the commotion. He appointed Marcus Aemilius Paullus as his master of the horse. The dictator, leading an army, defeated the Marsians in one battle and forced them into their fortified towns. He then captured Milionia, Plestina, and Fresilia over the course of a few days. Caius Lastorius and Marcus Metilius were sent as ambassadors to the consuls. Their instructions were to collect the remains of both armies and take every effort to prevent them from surrendering to the enemy, either out of fear or despair. This was a pressing concern after the Battle of Cannae. Ambassadors were sent to Hannibal to complain that Capua had been abandoned and was almost in the hands of the Romans. They urged him to provide assistance, as they were both besieged and surrounded by a defensive wall.

Those who retained their liberty and all their possessions who left before the Ides of March should be spared. However, those who left after that day, as well as those who remained, would be considered enemies. He failed in his attempt at Brundusium, so he changed direction. He expected the town to betray him, but his efforts were futile. Meanwhile, Campanian ambassadors arrived, bringing complaints and pleas for help. There was no opportunity for a conference, however, because many were suspected of disloyalty. The attention and observation of everyone were exercised, so that nothing like this could happen without being noticed.

After this, certain persons, covering themselves with nets in a fishing smack, were conveyed round to the Roman camp and conferred with the fugitives. This was frequently repeated by different parties, one after another, until eighty had been involved. However, once everything had been agreed upon for betraying the city, the plot was reported to Epicydes by Attalus, who felt hurt that he had not been entrusted with the secret. As a result, they were all put to death with torture.

The deserter suggested that the Syracusans were celebrating the festival of Diana; it was to last three days. During the siege, there was a deficiency of other things, so the feasts would be more profusely celebrated with wine, which was furnished by Epicydes for the people in general and distributed through the tribes by persons of distinction. The soldiers, as ordered, began to deploy the ladders. Meanwhile, a thousand heavily armed men moved stealthily towards the designated area in a narrow line. They awoke in their beds, and were put to the sword.

After this, the attack continued in every quarter, not secretly, but openly. They had now reached Epipolae, a place heavily guarded, where the aim was to intimidate the enemy, rather than avoid detection. Many inhabitants were unaware of the devastating event, as they were overwhelmed by wine and sleep. In a vast city, information from one area did not quickly spread throughout the entire city. When he realized every place around Epipolae was filled with armed men, he retreated to the Achradina after just teasing the enemy with a few missile discharges. He didn't march back out of fear for the number and strength of the enemy, but rather to see if any intestine treachery might reveal itself, taking advantage of the opportunity, and perhaps find the gates of the Achradina and island closed in the chaos.

As Marcellus gazed upon the city from the high ground, he beheld a place of unparalleled beauty. The city, renowned for its rich history, stirred within him a mix of emotions. He felt an overwhelming sense of pride and accomplishment, knowing that he had successfully breached its defenses. At the same time, he was moved by the city's storied past, which seemed to whisper tales of a bygone era. Marcellus, therefore, on the failure of this attempt, gave orders to retire to the Euryalus, which is an eminence at the extremity of the city. It is located at the farthest point from the sea and commands the road leading into the fields and the interior of the island, making it a conveniently situated location for introducing supplies. He brought back word to Marcellus that Philodemus had taken time to deliberate.

He posted guards and parties of troops at the gates of his camp to protect it from attack. The walls offered little protection, as they only surrounded the inner area, leaving the gates exposed to the surrounding streets. This was a deliberate design choice, allowing him to quickly respond to any potential threats while his soldiers pursued plunder elsewhere. After the arrangements were complete, the soldiers dispersed upon receiving the signal. They broke open doors and every place echoed with the sound of the alarm and chaos, but they restrained themselves from violence. They did not stop plundering until they had stripped the houses of all the property that had been accumulated over a long period of prosperity.

Philodemus, too, who had lost hope of receiving help, was assured he could return to Epicydes safely. He therefore withdrew the garrison and handed over the fortress to the Romans. The enemy produced more tumult than conflict. Crispinus not only drove back Hippocrates from his works but also pursued him as he fled with precipitation. Meanwhile, Marcellus drove Epicydes into the city. It was considered that enough had been done to prevent any danger arising in the future from their sudden sallies. They first fell ill and died from the unhealthy conditions of the season and climate. However, once the disease spread, it was mainly transmitted through direct contact with those already infected. As a result, those who contracted the illness either perished alone or drew others into their orbit, infecting them with the same severity of disease.

The violence of the plague was much greater in the Carthaginian army than in the Roman one. For the Romans, who had arrived at Syracuse earlier, had become accustomed to the local climate and water conditions over a longer period. The Roman army was severely affected by the outbreak. 27 Meanwhile, Bomilcar, who had gone a second time to Carthage, stated the condition of their allies in such a way that he inspired hope among them that they might not only provide effective aid but also make the Romans prisoners in the city they had captured. This induced the Carthaginians to send with him as many ships as possible, laden with all kinds of provisions, and to increase his fleet.

Setting sail from Carthage, he departed with 130 ships and 700 transports. He had tolerable winds for crossing to Sicily, but was thwarted by the same wind from reaching Cape Pachynum. The news of Bomilcar's approach sparked alternating fear and joy among both Romans and Syracusans. Intelligence of these events having been carried into the camp of the Sicilians, that Epicydes had departed from Syracuse, and the island was deserted by the Carthaginians, it was almost again delivered up to the Romans. After sounding the inclinations of the besieged in conferences, they sent ambassadors to Marcellus to treat about terms of surrender. They then invited persons entrusted with managing affairs by Epicydes to a conference. The Sicilians' army had sent them to Marcellus, instructing both those under siege and those not to share different fates. Neither was allowed to make separate arrangements for themselves.

Having put them to death, they summoned the multitude to an assembly. After complaining of the famine, which they had been accustomed to express in secret, they said, "Although we are pressed by so many calamities, we have no right to accuse Fortune, for it is at our own option how long we should continue to suffer these misfortunes." That, therefore, neither the city nor its inhabitants were in any danger, except from themselves, if they neglected an opportunity to restore themselves to the favour of the Romans. No such favourable opportunity would ever occur again as the one presented at this instant, immediately upon discovering they had been freed from their insolent tyrants. " 29 You have gained even benefits, whereas Hieronymus's frenzy brought ruin upon himself. "All things were obtainable and secure under Roman rule."

Having obtained an interview with Mericus in the absence of witnesses, he first explained to him the state in which he had left Spain, from which he had lately returned. "Everywhere," he said, "the Roman arms held sway; by serving the Romans, a man could gain great prestige among his fellow countrymen, whether he chose to serve with them or return home. If he chose to continue resisting the siege, his prospects would be bleak, surrounded as he was on all sides. Mericus was moved by these suggestions. Upon resolving to send ambassadors to Marcellus, he sent his brother among them. The brother, brought before Marcellus apart from the rest, received an assurance of protection via the same Spaniard. He arranged the method for carrying their object into effect and then returned to Achradina. Marcellus ordered a transport with armed men to be towed by a quadrireme to the Achradina at night. The soldiers were to be landed near the gate adjacent to the fountain of Arethusa.

During this confusion, some light ships that had been prepared beforehand and had sailed around landed a body of armed men on the island. These suddenly attacked the half-manned stations and the open door of the gate through which the troops had just run out moments before, gaining possession of the island with little resistance. The guards, fleeing in fear, abandoned their posts, allowing the attackers to seize control without opposition. Having summoned a council to which the Syracusans were invited, who among the Roman troops had been driven from home during the disturbances, Marcellus replied, "That the services rendered by Hiero over a period of fifty years were not more numerous than the injuries committed against the Roman people in these few years by those who had possessed Syracuse; but that most of these injuries had justly recoiled upon their authors, and that they had inflicted much more severe punishment upon themselves for violating treaties than the Roman people desired." The city was left open for the soldiers to plunder, following the placement of guards at each house belonging to those who had accompanied the Roman troops.

He returned to Lilybaeum on the third day after setting out, accompanied by a large number of ships carrying grain and spoils. The wisest course was to divide their forces and embrace the whole Spanish war. They arranged it so that Publius Cornelius would lead two-thirds of the Roman and allied troops against Mago and Hasdrubal. Meanwhile, Cneius Cornelius, with one-third of the original army and the added Celtiberians, would carry on the war with Barcine Hasdrubal. They did not consider it a serious offense; the goal was not to turn their arms against the Romans, as the reward they would receive for staying out of the war was substantial enough to compensate them for their involvement.

During the same period, an equal terror and a greater danger pressed upon Publius Scipio. Masinissa was a young man at that time; he was an ally of the Carthaginians. Later, the friendship of the Romans made him illustrious and powerful. He not only opposed himself with his Numidian cavalry to Scipio on his approach, but afterwards harassed him incessantly day and night. He did this so that he could cut off any stragglers who had gone out to a distance from the camp in search of wood and forage. He would ride up to the very gates of his camp and charge into the midst of his advanced guards, causing confusion throughout every quarter.

Leaving behind a small force under the command of Titus Fonteius, lieutenant-general, he set out at midnight. Meeting the enemy, he engaged in battle with them. The Romans, however, had the advantage, despite fighting irregularly. But the Numidian cavalry, whose presence the general believed he had escaped, suddenly surrounded him on both sides, causing great terror. After a new contest was entered into with the Numidians, a third enemy emerged, joining those already present, as the Carthaginian generals retreated to the rear while still engaged in battle.

The general, while fighting and encouraging his men, exposed himself wherever the strife was hottest. He was then struck by a lance on the right side of his body. As the enemy party, formed into a wedge, charged the troops gathered around him, they noticed Scipio falling lifeless from his horse. The Romans, elated with joy at this news, ran shouting through their entire line. After this, the Carthaginian generals, who were not slow in following up their victory, immediately after the battle, gave their soldiers little rest and hurried their army to Hasdrubal, son of Hamilcar; confidently hoping that uniting their forces with his would bring the war to a conclusion. They built a makeshift barrier around them, placing panniers filled with supplies at their feet. To reinforce this barrier, they added more panniers as needed, building up to its usual height. When additional panniers were scarce, they used whatever materials they had available – a collection of baggage and supplies of all kinds – to create a makeshift bulwark against the enemy.

Many men had fled to the neighboring woods in search of refuge. They escaped to the camp of Publius Scipio, who was commanded by Titus Fonteius. Cneius Scipio was killed in the 8th year after his arrival in Spain, and on the 29th day following his brother's death. The citizens' sorrow was partly alleviated by the loss of their armies, the alienation of the province, and the public disaster. In Spain, they mourned and regretted the generals themselves, particularly Cneius, who had been in command for a longer period and had first won their affections with his display of Roman justice and forbearance. 37

To his great talent had been added the discipline of Cneius Scipio, under whom he was thoroughly instructed over many years in all the qualifications of a soldier. The Roman knight in authority and honour among the troops was so transcendent that when a camp was fortified on this side of the Iberus, it was decided to elect a general from both armies in an assembly of the soldiers. The new commander would relieve each other in the guard of the rampart and keep the outposts until every soldier had given their vote. In this unanimous decision, Lucius Marcius was chosen as the supreme commander. They immediately turned their grief into anger and rushed to arms. Fueled by intense fury, they charged towards the gates, advancing in a disorganized and chaotic manner.

Lest the suddenness of the affair and the fear of night should frustrate a measure ill adapted to his condition, he thought it right that his soldiers be addressed and exhorted. Having called an assembly, he spoke as follows: "Soldiers, either my veneration for our late commanders, both living and dead, or our present situation, may impress on every one the belief that this command, though highly honourable to me and conferred by your suffrages, is also a heavy and anxious charge." Since you had dared to take action on your own, I intend to test how far you will go when authorized by your commander. Yesterday, when I signaled for retreat while you pursued the retreating enemy with great haste, I did not want to break your spirit but rather reserve it for greater glory and more advantageous opportunities. This way, when you are prepared and armed, you can seize an opportunity to attack your enemy when they least expect it, caught off guard, unarmed, and even sleeping. Men are most vulnerable to the threats they do not fear. This is because they fail to take precautions for things they consider insignificant.

The enemy fears nothing more than that we, who were recently besieged and attacked, might now launch an aggressive attack on their camp. Let that carnage be made among men, torpid with sleep, terrified at the unexpected tumult, and overpowered while lying defenseless in their beds. If at the critical moment, the chance to seize something that is short-lived arrives, and you hesitate even for an instant, then you will be unable to find it later when it has passed.

The enemy can be defeated when they are separated and divided. Let us wait for the coming night. Even if anyone had managed to escape that carnage, the Romans, after capturing the nearest camp, rushed over to the other with such haste that it would have been impossible for anyone to arrive before them with news of the disaster. At their ease and unguarded, the Romans, still hot from the recent battle and flushed with victory, launched an attack. As a result, no effective opposition could be made to them at the gates. Within the gates, troops from every part of the camp rushed together at the first shout and alarm, and a fierce conflict ensued. It would have lasted for a long time had not the bloody appearance of Roman shields revealed to the Carthaginians that their other forces were defeated, striking them with dismay.

They say that while he was haranguing his men, a stream of fire poured from his head without him perceiving it, causing great terror among the surrounding soldiers. A shield called the Marcian, with an image of Hasdrubal on it, remained in the temple until the time of the Capitol's burning, serving as a monument to his victory over the Carthaginians. After this, affairs remained in a tranquil state in Spain for a considerable time. Both parties, having given and received significant defeats, hesitated to risk a general battle. 40 During his transactions in Spain, Marcellus, after capturing Syracuse, settled other affairs in Sicily with great honor and integrity, adding to both his own reputation and the majesty of the Roman people. He then conveyed to Rome the city's ornaments, including its statues and pictures.

Those who had been induced to submit through fear after the capture of Syracuse were granted laws by the conqueror. The Romans still had a war of considerable magnitude at Agrigentum, led by Epicydes and Hanno, generals from the late war. A new third force was sent by Hannibal to replace Hippocrates, a Libyphoenician from Hippo, known as Mutines; an energetic man well-versed in all aspects of warfare under Hannibal's tutelage. A mutiny broke out among the Numidians in the camp, prompting him to leave. About 300 of them fled to Heraclea Minoa. He then set out to calm them down and persuade them to return. During his absence, he warned the generals not to engage with the enemy.

Both the generals were indignant at this conduct. Hanno, however, was particularly disturbed by it, as he had been previously concerned about his own reputation. "It is to be borne that a mongrel African should impose restraints on me, a Carthaginian general, commissioned by the Senate and the People." Epicydes, who wished to wait, was persuaded by him to agree to cross the river and offer battle. For, he said, if they waited for the mutineers and the battle ended in success, the mutineers would undoubtedly receive the credit for it. 41 While marshalling his army, ten Numidians rode up rapidly from the enemy's line with information that their countrymen, first induced by the same causes which brought on the mutiny, had retired to Heraclea. Additionally, because they saw their commander, who was about to engage in battle, had been sent out of the way by generals seeking to diminish his reputation, they refused to participate in the fight. The spirits of the Romans were increased by intelligence conveyed swiftly through the lines. The enemy was abandoned by the cavalry, which the Romans principally feared. At the same time, the enemy became dispirited, not only due to the loss of their main strength but also because they feared being attacked by their own cavalry.

The Numidians who stood quiet in the wings during the action, when they saw their party turning its back, accompanied them only for a short time. However, when they realized that all were fleeing towards Agrigentum with great haste, they turned away from the neighbouring towns to avoid being besieged. This was the last battle Marcellus fought in Sicily, after which he returned victorious to Syracuse. The year had almost come to a close; therefore, the senate decreed that Publius Cornelius, the praetor, should send a letter to Capua to the consuls. The directions were to instruct one of them, if they deemed it fitting, to travel to Rome while Hannibal was at a distance and nothing of great significance was occurring in Capua, to elect new magistrates.

After Lucius Cornelius Lentulus, Marcus Cornelius Cethegus, Caius Sulpicius, and Caius Calpurnius Piso became praetors. Although some held the view that the Senate's opinion should be sought on the matter, it was deemed wiser to delay the discussion until after the arrival of the horsemen who had brought a letter from Marcus. After the horsemen were dismissed, it was the first thing the consuls brought before the senate. The opinions of all present agreed, and it was decided that the plebeian tribunes should be instructed to consult with the commons as soon as possible, regarding whom they might send into Spain to take command of the army led by Cneius Scipio.

Liberty was not equally shared at Rome between the rich and the poor, nor among the nobles and the common people. " 3 The accused shifted the blame from himself to his soldiers. He claimed that because they had demanded battle in a turbulent manner, they were led into the field not on the day they wanted, but on the following day, when it was already evening. However, he argued that this was still a suitable time and favourable ground for them. Nevertheless, he maintained that either the enemy's reputation or strength was so great that they could not hold their position. Cneius Fulvius, therefore, before the day appointed for the comitia arrived, he went into exile to Tarquinii. The commons resolved that this was a legal exile. 4

Meanwhile, all the strength of the war was directed against Capua. It was, however, more strictly blockaded than besieged. A Numidian was eventually found, who, on undertaking to make his way with it, was charged with a letter; and going out by night, through the midst of the Roman camp, in order to fulfill his promise, he inspired the Campanians with confidence to try the effect of a sally from every quarter while they still had any strength remaining.

In the many encounters that followed, their cavalry were generally successful, but their infantry were defeated. However, it was not as joyful to conquer as it was miserable to be worsted in any respect by a besieged and nearly subjugated enemy. As soon as they came within range, on a signal given, the light troops leaped down. A line of infantry formed out of the body of horse, and suddenly rushed upon the cavalry of the enemy. The infantry discharged their javelins one after another with great rapidity; the javelins were thrown in large numbers onto men and horses indiscriminately, wounding a great many. The sudden and unexpected nature of the attack occasioned even greater terror. The cavalry, charging in a state of panic, pursued them with great slaughter all the way to their gates.

Quintus Navius is credited with advising the integration of infantry with cavalry. He received recognition from the general for this contribution. 5 Hannibal was perplexed between two objectives: capturing the citadel of Tarentum and retaining control of Capua. His concern for Capua, however, prevailed. He saw that the attention of all bodies, both allies and enemies, was fixed on him. His fate would be regarded as a proof of the consequences resulting from defection from Rome.

He left behind a significant portion of his baggage among the Bruttians and all his heavily armed troops. With this, he took with him a selected body of infantry and cavalry, the best he could choose for rapid march, and set his course towards Campania. As he achieved the standards of the Spanish, javelins were hurled at him from all directions, and nearly the entire line turned against him. However, neither the sheer number of his foes nor the ferocity of their attack could deter this hero's advance. 6 Lucius Portius Licinus and Titus Popilius, the lieutenant-generals, who commanded the camp, defended the rampart valiantly.

The ardour of the Romans was also dampened by General Appius Claudius, who received a wound; he was struck by a javelin in the upper part of his breast, beneath the left shoulder, while encouraging his men before the front line. A great number of the enemy were slain before the gate, and the rest were driven into the city in disorder. Regardless of how the battle commenced or terminated, this was the last fight before Capua's surrender.

As he pondered his next move, a sudden urge struck him to launch a surprise attack on Rome, the city at the heart of the conflict. The opportunity for him to accomplish his long-coveted object, which arose after the Battle of Cannae, had been overlooked and widely criticized by others. He, however, did not dispute this. By means of presents, he induced a Numidian who was ready to attempt anything, however daring, to take charge of a letter. He then entered the Roman camp under the guise of a deserter, with the intention of passing out privately on the other side and going to Capua.

His departure, which would be beneficial to them, would have drawn off the Roman generals and armies from the siege of Capua to the defense of Rome. When informed that enough troops existed to transport his entire army across in one night, following the provision of supplies for ten days, he led his legions to the river under cover of darkness. 8 Fabius Maximus believed it would be deeply shameful to leave Capua, and risk being intimidated and manipulated by Hannibal's threats. It was improbable that he, who had won a great victory at Cannae but showed no courage to besiege the city, would now, after being defeated in Capua, harbor ambitions to conquer Rome.

The army, conveyed across by rafts, continued its march uninterrupted. A liberal supply of provisions had been prepared for Fulvius, not only in the towns but also along the sides of the road. His men, filled with activity, urged each other to quicken their pace, remembering that they were defending their country. Quintus Fulvius, the proconsul, had set out from Capua with an army. The senate decreed that he should have equal authority with the consuls, so that if he entered the city, his power would not cease upon entry. Fulvius Flaccus entered the city with his troops through the Capuan gate. He then proceeded through the city's center and passed through Carinae before heading to Esquiliae. From there, he set up camp between the Esquiline and Colline gates.

Hannibal moved his camp to the Anio, three miles from the city. He secured his position there and then advanced with 2,000 horsemen from the Colline gate as far as the temple of Hercules. As he rode up, he took in as much of a view as possible of the walls and the city's layout. After the fight had begun, the consuls ordered the Numidian deserters who were on the Aventine, numbering twelve hundred, to march through the city's midst to the Esquiliae. They judged that no troops were better suited to fight among the hollows, garden walls, and tombs, or in the enclosed roads surrounding the city on all sides. The streets were filled with crowds of rustic villagers and cattle, driven into the city by the sudden alarm.

Several tumults arose during the remainder of the day and into the following night. 11 The storm subsided, leaving the armies in an uneasy silence. The important thing was that while he lay with his armed troops near the walls of the city, he was informed that troops had marched out of it with colours flying as a reinforcement for Spain. This was of less importance than what he learned from one of his prisoners: that the very ground on which his camp stood was being sold at this time without any decrease in its price.

After Hannibal's departure, vast heaps of brass were found there, as soldiers, driven by a religious feeling, had thrown in pieces of uncoined brass. Although the siege continued with undiminished vigor during his absence, Capua felt the return of Flaccus, and astonishment was aroused that Hannibal had not returned with him. Later, they discovered through conversations that they were abandoned and deserted, and that the Carthaginians had given up all hope of retaining Capua.

A proclamation was made by the proconsul, in accordance with a Senate decree, which was published among the enemy. Any Campanian citizen who arrived before a designated date would receive compensation. No one came over, for they were bound together by fear rather than loyalty. Their past transgressions during the revolt were too severe to warrant forgiveness. The nobility had abandoned the state, and they could not be persuaded to gather in the senate. The office of chief magistrate was held by a man who had not earned honor through his position, but instead diminished the office's power and authority with his own lack of merit.

They addressed a letter to Hannibal, charging him with "delivering Capua into the hands of the enemy, along with treacherously abandoning themselves and their troops to every species of torture." The letter also stated that he had gone off to the Bruttians in order to avoid being present when Capua fell, whereas the Romans remained at the siege of Capua, even when their city was attacked. It was then that victories were won at Cannae and Trasimene; by uniting, they pitched their camp near that of the enemy, and tested their luck. A letter to this effect was sent to some Numidians who had already agreed to render their services for a stated reward. He initially denied knowing her, displaying considerable determination. However, as the instruments of torture were revealed and prepared, he eventually admitted the truth.

Above seventy of those who were arrested, along with the late deserters, were flogged with rods. After having their hands cut off, they were forced to return to Capua. While the rest contended for sending ambassadors to the Roman generals, Vibius Virrius, who had been the instigator of the revolt from the Romans, observed that those who spoke of sending ambassadors and peace, and surrender, did not consider what they would do if they had the Romans in their power, or what they themselves must expect to suffer. Since the gods have deemed it proper, though I should not shrink from death, I remain free and master of myself. I possess the power to choose a death that is both honorable and gentle, thereby avoiding the tortures and indignities my enemies seek to inflict upon me.

I will not see Appius Claudius and Quintus Fulvius in the pride and insolence of victory. Nor will I be dragged through Rome as a spectacle in a triumph. Afterwards, I will be imprisoned in a dungeon or tied to a stake. My back will be lacerated with stripes. Then, I will place my neck under a Roman axe. For those who wish to submit to fate, I shall provide a feast before they encounter such atrocities. Twenty-seven senators followed Vibius Virrius to his home, where they joined him for a banquet. After attempting to distract themselves with wine, they succumbed to the impending doom and took the poison.

They then rose from the banquet, gave each other their right hands, and shared a final embrace. Merging their tears, they mourned both their own and their country's fate. Some stayed behind, so they could be burned together on the same pyre, while others returned home. Their veins being filled as a result of what they had eaten and the wine they drank made the poison less effective in hastening death. Consequently, although most of them suffered throughout the night and into the following day, all of them eventually died before the gates were opened to the enemy. The Roman Senate.

Fulvius argued that they should not take the risk of being accused by their loyal allies of questionable behavior, nor be subject to informers who showed no interest in their actions or words. He stated that he needed to halt and prevent any further investigation. They separated after this conversation. Appius was certain that his colleague, despite expressing himself warmly, would still wait for a letter from Rome, given the importance of the matter.

He then proceeded at full speed to Cales. Upon taking his seat on the tribunal, Campanians who had been brought forth were being bound to the stake. An express arrived from Rome, bearing a letter from Caius Calpurnius, the praetor, and a decree of the senate. Capua should be inhabited solely as a city; there should be no body politic, nor an assembly of the senate or people, nor any magistrates. Together with this advantage, a reputation for clemency was also obtained among the allies. The preservation of a city of great celebrity and opulence earned them this distinction. If that city had been demolished, all Campania and its neighboring people would have mourned its loss. This would have shown their enemies that the Romans could punish faithless allies, demonstrating their ability to mete out punishment. It also highlighted how little help Hannibal's protection of his own allies could offer in defending those he had taken under his wing. 17

As soon as it was dark, Hasdrubal ordered his heaviest force to exit the defile by any means possible. Several days were spent in openly discussing conditions, while many nights were spent privately sending Carthaginian troops out of their camp. After the greater part of the army had been sent out, he did not even stick to the terms he had proposed for himself; his sincerity decreased with his fears, and they became less and less agreed upon. Skirmishes did occur, but only at the rear of the Carthaginians and not with the advanced guard of the Romans. 18

During the time when these events took place, neither the states of Spain that had revolted after their defeat by the Romans, nor any other states, returned to Rome. At Rome, the attention of the Senate and the people was focused more intently on Italy than on Spain following the recovery of Capua. They were not, however, so clear as to the person whom they should send. Two generals had fallen within the space of thirty days; therefore, he who was to supply their places should be selected with unusual care.

The people afflicted by this fate, nearing desperation, converged on Campus Martius on election day. As they turned to face the magistrates, they surveyed the countenances of their most distinguished leaders, who were intensely engaged in conversation, their faces etched with concern. "Our situation is dire," they murmured in unison, "the state of our affairs and that of the commonwealth are at an all-time low. No one dares take on the command in Spain." After the business was concluded, and the passion and urgency of their enthusiasm had faded, a sudden silence fell, and a private contemplation of what they had done, whether their bias had clouded their judgment. They chiefly regretted his youth, but some were terrified by the fortune that attended his house and his name. While the two families to which he belonged were in mourning, he was heading into a province where he would conduct his operations among the tombs of his father and uncle. 19

Perceiving the solicitude and anxiety that people felt after performing the business with such ardor, he summoned an assembly to discuss his years, the command entrusted to him, and the war he had to carry on. In a noble and high-minded manner, he spoke about rekindling and renewing the waning enthusiasm and inspiring the people with more confident hopes than those based on human professions or reasoning about the promising appearance of affairs usually engenders. After that, he began to respond to the embassies of several states, which had been on hold due to the many challenges of the war.
